codeReview.summary: The fix still does not thread effective project/group ProviderRoute policy into the real worker runtime. Runtime provider construction remains hard-coded to `default`/`secondary`, and the added coverage still does not prove that changing policy changes actual provider selection end-to-end.

issues.description: The worker still seeds platform defaults with `ProviderRoute: "default"`, creates the primary provider with `RouteName: "default"`, the secondary provider with `RouteName: "secondary"`, and wraps them with `llm.NewFallbackProvider(..., "default", ..., "secondary")`. No runtime path reads `rules.Load(...).EffectivePolicy.ProviderRoute` and selects providers from that effective policy, so configured project/group provider-route policy still cannot change real runtime provider choice end-to-end.

`TestProviderRouteSelection` still only asserts that a fake rules loader returns `EffectivePolicy.ProviderRoute = "project-route"`; it never exercises `cmd/worker` or `llm.Processor` wiring to prove that different effective policies produce different real provider selections. The surrounding fallback/rate-limit tests instantiate providers with explicit route names, so they also do not verify policy-to-runtime threading.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The `go-reliability` skill focuses on failure-mode tests, but this feature required end-to-end runtime wiring verification across rules loading and worker/provider construction. The shipped tests stayed at metadata/unit level and missed the runtime contract, so the shared procedure does not appear to push mixed reliability/integration features to prove policy-to-runtime behavior explicitly.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: `.factory/skills/go-reliability/SKILL.md` emphasizes failure-mode tests, while `internal/llm/provider_test.go:150` only checks `EffectivePolicy.ProviderRoute` metadata and `cmd/worker/main.go:48-89` still hard-codes route names in runtime provider construction.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The project architecture note already records that directory-scoped `REVIEW.md` is still modeled as a single global winner, but there is no similar shared-state note that provider-route policy is not yet wired into runtime provider construction. That missing note makes it easier for follow-up workers to assume provider routing already works because `EffectivePolicy.ProviderRoute` exists in rule loading.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: `.factory/library/architecture.md` documents the REVIEW.md global-winner limitation, but no comparable library note calls out that `cmd/worker/main.go` still hard-codes provider routes instead of consuming `rules.LoadResult.EffectivePolicy.ProviderRoute`.

summary: Review failed: I checked the prior failed review, original handoff, current fix-state code, diff history, transcript evidence, and shared-state docs. The original failure remains unresolved because runtime provider routing is still hard-coded in worker wiring and the tests still do not prove policy-driven runtime provider selection end-to-end.
